coredata.dc:title: Protein-derived carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in sulfuric acid solution

coredata.dc:description: At present, the development of eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors (CIs) is considered an important and attractive method for controlling the corrosion of metals in acidic media. In this study, protein-derived carbon dots (P-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal reaction using protein as the raw material, and the corrosion inhibition performances of P-CDs for carbon steel (CS) were investigated systematically. The electrochemical test results showed that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 500 mg/L P-CDs on CS reached 95.1 % in 0.5 M H2SO4, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency was enhanced to 98.1 % after 8 h of immersion time. The composition and characterization of P-CDs was analysed using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet Spectroscopy (UV), X-ray Diffraction (XRD) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization showed that the surface of CS with P-CDs was smoother than that without P-CDs, and that P-CDs was able to adsorb on the surface of CS to form a protective layer, thereby inhibiting corrosion. Adsorption studies indicate that P-CDs form a protective layer on the surface of CS through both physisorption and chemisorption. Overall, these findings demonstrate that P-CDs exhibit superior protective effects against the corrosion of CS in 0.5 M H2SO4.

head.ce:title: Protein-derived carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in sulfuric acid solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, the development of eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors (CIs) is considered an important and attractive method for controlling the corrosion of metals in acidic media. In this study, protein-derived carbon dots (P-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal reaction using protein as the raw material, and the corrosion inhibition performances of P-CDs for carbon steel (CS) were investigated systematically. The electrochemical test results showed that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 500 mg/L P-CDs on CS reached 95.1 % in 0.5 M H2SO4, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency was enhanced to 98.1 % after 8 h of immersion time. The composition and characterization of P-CDs was analysed using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet Spectroscopy (UV), X-ray Diffraction (XRD) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization showed that the surface of CS with P-CDs was smoother than that without P-CDs, and that P-CDs was able to adsorb on the surface of CS to form a protective layer, thereby inhibiting corrosion. Adsorption studies indicate that P-CDs form a protective layer on the surface of CS through both physisorption and chemisorption. Overall, these findings demonstrate that P-CDs exhibit superior protective effects against the corrosion of CS in 0.5 M H2SO4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the financial losses caused by metal corrosion are significant, reaching $2.5 trillion worldwide in 2013 [1]. More widely applied inorganic acids such as H2SO4, HCl, HNO3, and H3PO4 are used for industrial pickling, acidizing, and the chemical industry [2–6]. Carbon steel (CS) is one of the widely used metal materials in industry. Because of its superior ductility, thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity and weldability, it plays an important role in metal smelting, ships, machinery and equipment, building structures, water supply systems, petroleum and electric power industries, and it is susceptible to corrosion in acidic environments. In acidic environments, metals are threatened by corrosion, and it is essential to take measures to prevent metal corrosion [7,8]. To overcome this challenge, the introduction of corrosion inhibitors (CIs) has become the main method of corrosion prevention. Based on their composition, CIs are classified into inorganic and organic CIs [9]. Among them, inorganic CIs include chromates, tungstates, molybdates, phosphates and nitrites. Inorganic CIs can make aluminium, steel, magnesium and other metal surfaces passivation, has a good corrosion inhibition effect, but the environment and human toxicity is greater and is limited to use [10]. With the enhancement of the awareness of environmental protection and sustainable development, inorganic CIs with high toxicity are gradually replaced by organic CIs [11].

Researchers have studied organic CIs containing heteroatoms such as N, O, P and aromatic structures, such as ionic liquids, imidazolines, amines and quaternary ammonium salts have attracted much attention [12]. Compared with inorganic CIs, organic CIs are characterized by small dosage, low toxicity and high corrosion inhibition efficiency [13]. However, organic CIs still have problems such as complicated preparation process, high cost and toxic reagents [14,15]. Although many inorganic and organic compounds can be used as CI, their large-scale application is difficult due to their toxicity and environmental pollution. Another globally problem is food waste, but the biomass within it can provide a renewable source of CI [16]. The protein contained in food waste is made up of amino acids, which contain a large amount of N, S, and C. It is considered a potential green CI for protecting metals from corrosion [17]. Therefore, the research on novel eco-friendly and high-performance CI has attracted the attention of researchers around the world [18,19].

In recent years, CDs have attracted increasing attention due to their outstanding electron transport performance, optical properties, and biocompatibility [20–22]. The low-cost, light-emitting, micro-sized material has been extensively studied [23,24]. The small particle size of CDs and the aggregation of nanoscale CIs on the surface of metal materials by self-agglomeration are conducive to the formation of a denser protective film, which has a higher protective performance compared with other organic and inorganic CIs. At the same time, researchers focused on CDs with unique potential and explored their applications in the field of corrosion inhibition. Cao et al. [25] conducted in-depth studies on the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs prepared by citric acid and urea for CS in HCl solution by using surface topography analysis and electrochemical method. Padhan et al. [26] synthesized N-CDs doped with Cu to inhibit the corrosion of Q235 steel in HCl solution. The corrosion of the anode and the hydrogen evolution reaction of the cathode were inhibited by covering the metal surface. The study revealed that Cu-doped N-CDs have superior corrosion inhibition performance compared to N-CDs. In addition, Cui and Li [27] prepared CDs by hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid and thiourea in a high-temperature reactor. They discovered that CDs doped with N and S exhibited higher inhibition performance than CDs without these elements. In another work, Zeng et al. [28] prepared Me-CDs by hydrothermal method. The Me-CDs can be adsorbed steadily on the surface of CS and effectively inhibit its corrosion. These works have demonstrated the potential application of CDs in the field of corrosion inhibition, providing a new method in developing efficient and eco-friendly CI [29].

This work focuses on the application of protein-derived carbon dots (P-CDs) as eco-friendly CI for CS in H2SO4. Hence, the use of food waste biomass as raw material not only provides a sustainable solution for corrosion prevention, but also reduces the environmental impact of waste treatment. The innovation of this work lies in the preparation of P-CDs by using animal waste tissues, which not only makes the preparation process more environmentally friendly and sustainable, and has certain economic advantages over other CIs, but also provides a new way for the effective use of resources. The inhibition process of P-CDs on CS was studied by electrochemical technique, weight loss test and surface analysis. This work provides valuable insight into the inhibitory mechanism of P-CDs on CS and suggests promising direction for the application of P-CDs in materials science. It is believed that this work can provide a sustainable and eco-friendly anti-corrosion strategy for industrial production.

The H2SO4 was purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co, LTD and the absolute ethanol was purchased from Chengdu Colon Chemical Co, LTD. The composition of CS used are as follows: C 0.160 %, Si 0.080 %, Mn 0.210 %, P 0.015 %, S 0.012 %, and the rest are Fe. The CS has been cut into 1.0 cm cubes, covered with epoxy resin, and preserves an exposed area of 1.0 cm2. Before each experiment, the CS was successively polished with sandpaper of 100–7000#. After repeated rinsing with deionized water and ethanol, the electrochemical test was carried out.

In this study, the preparation process of P-CDs was modified from the method reported by Wang, Xu et al. [30,31]. The preparation procedure of P-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The raw material used for the preparation of P-CDs was chicken. At first, the fat is removed from the chicken protein and after washing it is crushed using a grinder. It is then degreased in absolute ethanol at 313 K. After this, the samples are centrifuged, the pH is adjusted to 9.5 and heated in a water bath for 3 h. The protein is obtained by centrifugation of the sample. Then, 5 g of protein and 50 mL of deionized water were added to the polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) inner tank and heated at 473 K for 6 h. At the end of the reaction, the precipitate was removed by centrifugation at 10,000 r/min and then filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was purified in a dialysis bag with a molecular interception of 1000 for 24 h. Finally, the obtained liquid was placed in a freeze-drying oven to obtain a brown powder. The prepared sample was dispersed in deionized water and exposed to an UV light source, and the sample displayed as blue, which proved that the prepared P-CDs presented the typical features of carbon dots.

The P-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy. The functional groups in P-CDs were analysed by FTIR (Nicolet IS10, USA) between 400−1 to 4000 cm−1. The fluorescence emission spectra of P-CDs before and after soaking CS in H2SO4 were measured by UV–vis (Thermo Scientific Evolution 300). The elemental state of P-CDs on the CS surface was analysed using XPS (ThermoFisher K-Alpha) and C1s binding energy as the energy standard. Furthermore, the morphology of P-CDs was observed by TEM (FEI Tecnai G12).

During the weight loss experiment, CS samples with sizes of 3.0 cm × 2.0 cm × 1.0 cm were used. The samples were submerged in 0.5 M H2SO4 containing different concentrations P-CDs (50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 200 mg/L, 500 mg/L) and at different temperatures (298 K, 308 K, 318 K, 328 K) for 6 h. After immersion, the CS sample was rinsed with deionized water and dried. Finally, electronic balance is used to determine the weight loss before and after immersion. And the inhibition efficiencies are calculated using these weight loss values.

Electrochemical measurements are conducted using CHI660E workstations and employing standard three-electrode systems. The CS electrode was used as the working electrode (WE). The platinum sheet (surface area 4.0 cm2) and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter electrode (CE) and reference electrode (RE), respectively. To ensure that the working electrode reaches a stable state, it is necessary to immerse the WE for 1800 s first for open circuit potential (OCP) test. To record EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) data, the signal frequency range is 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz and the amplitude of the signal is ±10 mV (vs. OCP) of sinusoidal voltage. The data fitting and analysis of the EIS was carried out using ZSimpWin software. Finally, the Tafel test was performed in the range ± 250 mV (vs. OCP) at a scanning speed of 1.00 mV/s.

Prior to surface analysis, the CS sample is polished with sandpaper graded from 100 to 7000# until the surface is mirror-like smoothness. Next, the CS is rinsed with deionized water and placed in ethanol for 5 min before drying. After that, the CS sample was immersed in H2SO4 solution with 500 mg/L P-CDs at 298 K for 6 h. The surface morphology and elemental composition of the CS samples were investigated by SEM, EDS and AFM. The microstructure of the CS before and after the addition of P-CDs can be observed. Through these analyses, the morphology and composition of CS can be observed before and after treatment with P-CDs.

Fig. 2a shows the FTIR spectra of P-CDs. The absorption peaks are observed at 3205 cm−1, 2963 cm−1, 1674 cm−1, 1458 cm−1, 1403 cm−1, 1113 cm−1, 668 cm−1 and 533 cm−1. The stretching vibrations with O − H or N − H absorption peaks in P-CDs were observed at 3406 cm−1 and 3205 cm−1 [32]. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 2963 cm−1 relates to the stretching vibration of −CH2 or − CH3 [9,33]. The peaks at 1674 cm−1 and 1458 cm−1 are the stretching vibration of CO or C − N [34]. The peaks at 1404 cm−1 and 1113 cm−1 correspond to the flexural vibration of C − H and the tensile vibration of C − O, respectively [35,36]. The absorption peak at 668 and 533 cm−1 is the result of the C − S and C − NH. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the P-CDs were measured by UV. In Fig. 2b, it is shown that P-CDs present absorption peaks at 211 nm, 279 nm and 343 nm. Of these, the main absorption at 211 nm is the π-π* transition of CC, and the absorption at 279 nm and 343 nm is the n-π* transition of CO [37]. It can therefore be concluded that P-CDs contain numerous unsaturated bonds. Through comparison of the absorption peak of P-CDs before and after soaking CS in H2SO4, it can be seen that the absorption peak is low after immersion. It indicates that P-CDs are adsorbed on the surface of CS. The XRD pattern in Fig. 2c shows that the P-CDs have a graphite-like structure. There is a diffraction peak near 20° (2θ) and the position of this diffraction peak corresponds to the crystallographic diffraction peak at the surface in the graphite-like material structure, further confirming the presence of graphite-like structure in the core of CDs. The position of the peak is shifted to the left and broadened, which may be due to the presence of N, O, S and functional groups in the P-CDs [23]. In Fig. 2d, the TEM image showed clearly the morphology and size of P-CDs, which appeared as a spherical substance with a size of about 15 nm. In conclusion, P-CDs might contain aminos, carboxyl, benzene and other functional groups. It means that P-CDs can adsorb on the surface of CS by these functional groups or heteroatoms and effectively inhibit its corrosion process.

The weight loss test provides an obvious and effective method of evaluating the efficacy of CI. The following equation can be used to calculate the corrosion rate of CS and corrosion inhibition efficiency ( η ) of P-CDs [38–40]:
(1)

(2)

In Eq. (1), v represents the corrosion rate; ΔW is the lost mass; S is the surface area of CS. t is the immersion time. In Eq. (2), η represents the corrosion inhibition efficiency, and
V i
and
V o
represent the corrosion rate with and without P-CDs, respectively.

Fig. 3 represents v and η for CS with different concentrations of P-CDs and at different temperatures. It can be observed that the corrosion rate of CS decreases significantly as the concentration of P-CDs increases from 50 mg/L to 500 mg/L (Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, with increasing temperature from 298 K to 328 K, the η gradually decreases (Fig. 3b). Table 1 displays the v and η at various concentrations and temperatures. As shown in Table 1, 500 mg/L P-CDs exhibits maximum η of 96.4 %, indicating that P-CDs acts as an effective CI.

Fig. 4a–b shows the OCP curves of CS in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions containing P-CDs at different concentrations and immersion times. With the immersion time of 1800 s, all the OCP curves tend to stabilise, indicating that a steady state is reached. The OCP values of CS in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing P-CDs are shifted in the positive direction compared to the blank solution, and the OCP values increase with the increase of P-CDs concentration. In addition, the OCP values were shifted both in the positive and negative direction with the increase of immersion time. It shows that P-CDs adsorbed on the surface of CS and prevented the contact with water molecules, and the deviation of the OCP value was less than 85 mV, which indicates that P-CDs belongs to the mixed type of CIs [19]. Compared to organic CIs, the steady state of P-CDs takes a longer time to reach, which may be due to the slower transfer rate of P-CDs because of their larger particle size and heavier weight [41]. There are good reasons to believe that P-CDs can be used as hybrid CIs, effectively blocking the active sites and inhibiting both anodic and cathodic reactions.

Different immersion times also have a significant effect on the corrosion current density of CIs. For corrosion solutions with different immersion times, the corrosion current density of P-CDs decreased at 1–8 h, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency increased with time. Different immersion times showed lower corrosion current density. It indicated that both anodic desorption and cathodic reduction were inhibited to different degrees when P-CDs were present. Compared with the blank solution, the maximum corrosion potential change of the solution containing P-CDs was less than ±85 mV, indicating that the P-CDs on the CS surface were all mixed CIs, which retarded both anodic and cathodic reactions.

denote the corrosion current density in the presence and absence of P-CDs, respectively. With the addition of P-CDs,

This is related to the desorption and thermal movement of the CI, as well as the anodic dissolution of the metal atoms encapsulated by it. There is no significant desorption of the P-CDs CI, which may be due to the strong adsorption of the P-CDs nanoparticles with the CS surface. This is in agreement with the analytical results of OCP, indicating that P-CDs have multiple adsorption centers and show significant inhibition on CS. In different concentrations, the concentration of P-CDs increased, the value of
i corr

η p
reached a maximum of 98.1 %. It shows that P-CDs have good corrosion inhibition performance. From Table 4 , it can be seen that the
η p
of P-CDs is relatively high compared to that in previous studies.

The main objective in the EIS analysis is to understand the corrosion mechanism of P-CDs on CS in 0.5 M H2SO4, and the experimental data obtained can be fitted with an equivalent circuit. The electrochemical Nyquist, Bode and equivalent circuit diagrams are shown in Fig. 5 to analyse the adsorption behaviour of the CI in the corrosive medium. With the addition of P-CDs, the capacitive arc resistance diameter increased significantly, indicating that P-CDs formed an effective adsorption film on the surface of CS and inhibited the corrosion of CS in this system. This may be due to the fact that the CS surface was covered by more CI adsorption as the concentration of P-CDs increased. It can be seen in Fig. 5a–b that all Nyquist plots exhibit incomplete semicircles. The semicircle consists of a high frequency region and a low frequency region. Since the time constants of the two capacitive arcs are similar, the capacitive arcs are superimposed so that the impedance plots show semicircular arcs. Where the high frequency circular arc is related to the charge transfer process, the low frequency induced arc is due to the relaxation process of ion adsorption [46]. The capacitive loop diameter of the P-CDs corrosion inhibition system was much larger than that of the blank condition over the entire range studied. The diameter expanded significantly with increasing P-CDs concentration, indicating an enhanced corrosion inhibition effect on CS. This suggests that the main reason for the controlled charge transfer process in the P-CDs CI system is that the adsorption of P-CDs on the surface of CS blocks the charge transfer pathway, thus inhibiting the corrosion of the electrode. In addition, at the same concentration, the diameter of the capacitance loop increased with the extension of the test time, indicating that the P-CDs CI was able to adsorb stably on the surface of CS and protect it from erosion.

As can be seen from the Bode plots, the phase angle frequency relationship curves show a narrow peak, indicating that the electrode reaction process has only one time constant (Fig. 5c–d). Compared with the blank solution, the phase angle peaks and impedance modulus in the plots tended to increase gradually after the addition of P-CDs CI. When in the same system, the frequency range of the phase angle becomes larger with increasing concentration and immersion time, indicating a shift to higher capacitance and a decrease in the corrosion process. The corrosion rate is inversely proportional to the impedance modulus (|Z|0.01) in the low-frequency region, and an increase in the impedance modulus leads to a more pronounced anti-corrosion effect. With the increase of P-CDs concentration and immersion time, |Z|0.01 showed an increasing trend. The results show that P-CDs adsorbed on the surface, formed a protective film, blocked the electron transfer process, increased the capacitive resistance, and could block the direct contact between corrosive substances and CS. It indicates that P-CDs played a protective role in sulfuric acid solution, improved the corrosion inhibition efficiency and prevented the corrosion of metal.

The gradual increase of the arc diameter and impedance modulus of the P-CDs and the beginning of the instability of the inductive circuit in the studied time range indicate the formation of corrosion products corresponding to the relaxation process occurring on the surface of the CS and the desorption of adsorbed substances from the surface. Therefore, two equivalent circuit models R(QR(LR)) and R(QR) were used to fit the EIS data to determine the electrochemical parameters, and the chi-square value (χ2) is the goodness of fit (Fig. 5e–f). The constant phase element ( CPE ) is used instead of the double layer capacitor (

Compared with the blank, P-CDs addition could protect the CS surface more effectively, which indicated that P-CDs favoured the generation of a barrier on the surface of CS with good inhibition performance. In addition, the inhibition efficiency increases with the increase of corrosion inhibition time. Compared with the corrosion inhibition performance of carbon dots previously reported in the literature listed in Table 4, a high inhibition efficiency of 97.3 % was obtained at 8 h immersion time, indicating that P-CDs can be used as an efficient type of CI for a long period of time.

Fig. 6 shows the AFM was used to observe the three-dimensional morphology of CS samples immersed in H2SO4 for 6 h at 298 K, with or without 500 mg/L P-CDs. The CS surface etched in H2SO4 solution without P-CDs (Fig. 6a). It is obvious that the surface of the CS is seriously damaged due to the corrosion by H2SO4, with evident gullies. However, the corrosion rate of CS was slowed down after it was immersed in H2SO4 solution which P-CDs for 6 h (Fig. 6c). As indicated in Fig. 6b and d, the average surface roughness of CS without P-CDs and with P-CDs is 95.20 nm and 38.52 nm, respectively. The corrosion rate of CS is reduced due to the protective layer formed by P-CDs on it. The AFM results were in agreement with the electrochemistry results, which confirmed that the presence of P-CDs effectively inhibited the corrosion of CS.

Fig. 7 presents the surface morphology and composition of CS after polished, attacked with H2SO4 and treated with P-CDs. The polished CS surface appears smooth and uniform, without observable corrosion products, and is mainly composed of C and Fe elements (Fig. 7a–b). Conversely, the CS exhibits corrosion in H2SO4 and generates significant numbers of corrosion products on its surface, showing irregular corrosion pits (Fig. 7c–d). Within these, the S element indicates that H2SO4 causes the corrosion reaction of CS. Fig. 7e–f reveals that the surface of the CS is relatively flat and the corrosion products are reduced after the application of P-CDs. It indicates that the introduction of P-CDs inhibits the corrosion process. The content of the elements C and O becomes higher to a greater extent due to the fact that a large number of oxygen-containing organic molecules in the P-CDs are adsorbed on the surface of the CS, the relative content of C and O increases more and the relative content of Fe decreases. The EDS results showed that the polished surface of the CS contains primarily C and Fe elements. After being corroded by H2SO4, the S element on the CS surface increases, which could be the SO4 2−.

In Fig. 8 the XPS spectra of CS immersed in H2SO4 with P-CDs for 12 h are presented. It can be seen in Fig. 8a, the surface of the CS contains mainly C, O, N, S and Fe elements. In Fig. 8b, C 1s high-resolution fitting spectral peaks are observed at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.5 eV, corresponding to CC, CO and C − N/C − S, respectively [41]. In Fig. 8c, the fitting results of O 1 s shows three peaks corresponding to O − C, −OH and O2−, respectively, indicating that P-CDs are adsorbed on the surface of CS [50,51]. The high-resolution fitting spectral peak for N 1 s reveals that the peak at 399.4 eV corresponds to the adsorption associated with N − Fe and P-CDs (Fig. 8d). S 2p peaks are detected at 168.5 eV and 169.5 eV, corresponding to Fe − S and SO4 2−, respectively (Fig. 8e). It is suggested that the surface of CS is attacked by H2SO4 [52,53]. There were six peaks in the Fe 2p spectrum, among which the low binding energy peak at 710.3 eV was the peak of FeOOH, and the peak at 712.3 eV was related to Fe2+ and Fe3+ [54]. The peak of Fe 2p1/2 at 719.7 eV proves that P-CDs adheres to the CS surface, and the other three peaks correspond to Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 [47,55]. These observations demonstrate that the adsorption of P-CDs on the surface of CS is caused by O, N and S elements. The formed protective film effectively inhibits the corrosion of CS.

The adsorption isotherm process was analysed based on the weight loss experimental results. Here, conventional adsorption isothermal models such as Langmuir, Temkin, Freundlich, EI-Awady, Frumkin and Flory-Huggins are used to investigate the interaction between P-CDs and CS surfaces. Fig. 9 presents the six different adsorption isotherms studied in this work. These isotherms could be calculated using the following equations [56–58]:

represents the concentration of P-CDs, and θ (η/100) denotes the surface coverage. The Langmuir model assumes that each molecule is only present at one adsorption site. The Temkin adsorption model takes into account the interaction forces between molecules. Frumkin adsorption isotherms demonstrate the presence of intermolecular interactions. The EI-Awady adsorption model considers the effect of the number of adsorbed layers. The Freundlich adsorption model is suitable for the same range as the Langmuir adsorption model. The Flory-Huggins adsorption model is designed to determine the density of the adsorption film and the number of displaced water molecules. In Fig. 9, the linear regression coefficient R2 of the Langmuir adsorption model is close to 1, indicating that Langmuir is the optimal adsorption model. In Table 7 , the
K ads

K ads
is related to the adsorption capacity of the CI. The larger the value of

, the stronger the adsorption capacity of the CI on the surface of CS [31]. In addition, the Gibbs free energy value for adsorption was calculated by Eq. (14) [43,59]:

This is due to the fact that an increase in the concentration of P-CDs increases the energy required for the oxidation reaction of the metal and therefore the adsorption of P-CDs on the surface of CS hinders the corrosion reaction and thus reduces the corrosion of iron [64]. The positive value of Δ

In addition,
E a
and Δ
H a
varied with CI concentration, which verified the thermodynamic relationship between
E a
and Δ
H a
[60].

Fig. 12 illustrates the corrosion inhibition mechanism of P-CDs for CS in H2SO4. In Fig. 12a, as previously reported, the H+ can adsorbed on the surface of CS can make the CS surface positively charged. Therefore, the SO4 2− is easily adsorbed on the CS surface [25,67]. In H2SO4, H+ readily diffuses to the surface of CS, causing serious corrosion of CS. As illustrated in Fig. 12b, the protonated N and S have electrostatic interactions on the steel surface after addition of P-CDs, which can replace the H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface of CS. The FTIR shows that the prepared P-CDs might contain numerous functional groups and unsaturated bonds, resulting in adsorption on the surface of CS. The XPS indicates that the N, S and O atoms in the P-CDs form covalent bonds with the CS surface, which enhances the adsorption on the steel surface. The generation of protective film with high coverage can prevent the diffusion of H+ to the surface of CS, and thereby inhibit the corrosion of CS.

In summary, the development of CIs such as high corrosion inhibition efficiency, low cost and environmentally friendly has become the focus of researchers. In many studies, researchers have used phenylenediamine, citric acid, and salicylic acid to prepare carbon dots CIs and study their corrosion inhibition performance on CS in acidic media. The results show that carbon dots have certain corrosion inhibition effect, but there are disadvantages such as long preparation time and low corrosion inhibition efficiency. Protein as raw material has low cost, which makes the preparation cost of P-CDs relatively low and has certain economic advantages compared with other CIs. Therefore, we successfully prepared novel P-CDs by using bio-waste proteins as precursors with a hydrothermal preparation time of 6 h. In order to explore the corrosion inhibition performance of the prepared P-CDs, a detailed study was carried out. Tafel curves confirmed that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the P-CDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with a concentration of 500 mg/L for an immersion time of 8 h was 98.1 %, which was a hybrid CIs mainly inhibiting the anode. EIS results showed that the P-CDs adsorbed on the surface of CS formed a barrier film, which effectively prevented the transfer of electrons, and the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency reached 97.3 %. Therefore, the prepared P-CDs are CIs with high corrosion inhibition performance. The adsorption of P-CDs on the CS surface followed Langmuir adsorption. Surface analysis showed that the surface of CS with P-CDs in solution was smoother than that without P-CDs. The adsorption mechanism showed that P-CDs adsorbed on the metal surface to form a dense protective film, which would reduce the corrosion of mild steel, effectively preventing the dissolution reaction of anodic iron and effectively inhibiting the reduction reaction of cathodic hydrogen ions. Overall, our work demonstrated the preparation of a new type of P-CDs with highly efficient corrosion inhibition performance. Therefore, through the innovative method of preparing protein-derived carbon dots from animal waste tissues, this study provides a new idea for the selection and preparation of carbon dots in the future, as well as a guide for the corrosion inhibition mechanism of P-CDs. It not only makes the preparation process more environmentally friendly and sustainable, but also provides a new way for the effective use of resources.
